description: The provided image displays a user-interface button labeled Check for updates with a red bookmark icon on a gray circular background, obscuring any underlying scientific figure. Consequently, no material microstructure, diffraction pattern, spectrum, or quantitative data plot can be directly observed or verified from the image pixels. All substantive scientific content regarding the low-density high-strength Fe-Mn-Al-C steel must be drawn from the accompanying text extraction summary, which reports isothermal hot compression experiments at 900–1150 °C and strain rates of 0.01–10 s⁻¹. The text states dynamic recrystallization activation energy of 669.844 kJ mol⁻¹, Zener-Hollomon parameter analysis, and grain size evolution ranging from 4 to 75 μm. It also describes a DRX state diagram delineating work hardening, partial DRX, and complete DRX zones, plus a grain size prediction model validated by finite element simulation. Because the figure content is unreadable, specific visual trends, axis scales, curve shapes, or microstructural features remain unconfirmed and are presented strictly as text-reported findings.

Figure 1 displays the initial microstructure of the experimental low-density high-strength Fe-Mn-Al-C steel (1.46 wt.% C, 25.14 wt.% Mn, 10 wt.% Al, 0.053 wt.% Nb, balance Fe), obtained using a LEXT OLS4000 laser confocal microscope after standard grinding, polishing, and etching with 30% nitric acid alcohol solution (volume fraction). The micrograph exhibits a polycrystalline grain structure with distinct grain boundaries and prominent annealing twins within the austenitic matrix, indicative of the as-homogenized and forged condition. Grain sizes are non-uniform across the field of view, ranging from relatively fine to coarse grains, with a scale bar of 100 μm for reference. This as-received microstructure establishes the critical baseline for subsequent isothermal hot compression studies performed on a Gleeble-3500 simulator at temperatures of 900–1150 °C and strain rates of 0.01–10 s⁻¹, enabling quantitative comparison of dynamic recrystallization kinetics, grain refinement, and microstructural evolution induced by thermomechanical processing.

The figure presents a temperature–time profile schematically illustrating the isothermal constant strain rate hot compression experimental procedure for Fe-Mn-Al-C steel (composition: 1.46 C, 25.14 Mn, 10 Al, 0.053 Nb, balance Fe) conducted on a Gleeble-3500 thermo-mechanical simulator using cylindrical specimens of Φ8 mm × 12 mm. The thermal cycle comprises: heating to 1200 °C at 10 °C/s, homogenization holding for 120 s, controlled cooling to the target deformation temperature (900–1150 °C) at 5 °C/s, an isothermal hold for 15 s to equalize internal and external temperature gradients, followed by compression to a maximum true strain of 0.92 (60% height reduction) at strain rates of 0.01, 0.1, 1, and 10 s⁻¹, and finally water quenching to retain the high-temperature deformed microstructure for subsequent metallographic analysis. The diagram explicitly labels each stage’s thermal parameter, emphasizing the 15-s holding step that ensures temperature uniformity prior to deformation and the quenching step that freezes the dynamic recrystallization state. This protocol underpins the rheological stress measurements and microstructural characterization used to construct constitutive models, DRX state diagrams, and grain size prediction models for the low-density high-strength steel.

This figure presents the rheological behavior and peak stress response of low-density high-strength Fe-Mn-Al-C steel under isothermal hot compression. Subfigure (a) shows true stress–true strain curves obtained at a constant strain rate of 0.01 s⁻¹ across deformation temperatures from 900 °C to 1150 °C, where peak stress decreases markedly with increasing temperature, dropping from approximately 305 MPa at 900 °C to about 54 MPa at 1150 °C. Subfigure (b) displays curves at a fixed temperature of 1100 °C for strain rates ranging from 0.01 to 10 s⁻¹, demonstrating strong positive strain-rate sensitivity with peak stress rising as strain rate increases. All stress–strain profiles exhibit a distinct peak followed by flow softening or steady-state behavior, characteristic of dynamic recrystallization (DRX). Subfigures (c) and (d) are 3D surface plots mapping peak stress as a function of temperature and strain rate, covering 0.01–0.1 s⁻¹ and 1–10 s⁻¹ respectively. Collectively, the data establish that the steel is negatively temperature-sensitive and positively strain-rate-sensitive, providing foundational rheological evidence for constitutive modeling and DRX kinetic analysis.

Figure 4 comprises two linear regression plots derived from the Arrhenius hyperbolic sine constitutive equation for low-density high-strength Fe-Mn-Al-C steel under isothermal hot compression. Subfigure (a) shows ln(strain rate) versus ln[sinh(ασ_p)] at temperatures from 900 °C to 1150 °C (black squares, red circles, blue triangles, magenta inverted triangles, green diamonds, and dark blue left triangles), giving an average slope k₁ = 5.135. Subfigure (b) shows ln[sinh(ασ_p)] versus 1000/T at strain rates of 0.01, 0.1, 1, and 10 s⁻¹ (black squares, red circles, blue triangles, and magenta inverted triangles), giving an average slope k₂ = 15.690. The non-parallel nature of the fitted lines indicates that deformation temperature and strain rate significantly influence the dynamic recrystallization (DRX) activation energy. These plots support the calculation of an average DRX activation energy of 669.844 kJ mol⁻¹ and the establishment of the Zener-Hollomon parameter, confirming that the steel exhibits negative temperature sensitivity and positive strain-rate sensitivity during hot deformation.

The figure presents a linear regression analysis for low-density high-strength Fe-Mn-Al-C steel subjected to isothermal hot compression at 1050 °C and a strain rate of 0.01 s⁻¹. It plots ln(σ/σp) on the vertical axis against [ln(ε/εp) + 1 − ε/εp] on the horizontal axis, where σp and εp denote peak stress and peak strain, respectively. Black cross markers represent discrete experimental data points, while the red solid line shows the linear fit. The regression yields a material constant C = 0.1461 with a correlation coefficient R = 0.9728, demonstrating a strong linear relationship. This fitting procedure, based on the Cingara-McQueen equation, enables extraction of the material constant required to determine the critical strain for dynamic recrystallization (DRX). The result supports the constitutive modeling of DRX kinetics and validates the applicability of this analytical approach for describing the rheological behavior of Fe-Mn-Al-C steel under the specified thermomechanical processing condition.

Figure 6 presents the functional relationships between characteristic rheological stress curve values and the Zener-Hollomon parameter (expressed as lnZ) for a low-density high-strength Fe-Mn-Al-C steel under hot deformation. The three subplots display double-Y-axis scatter plots with regression fits obtained via multiple linear regression: (a) critical stress σ_c in MPa (black stars, R = 0.947) and critical strain ε_c (red circles, R = 0.911); (b) peak stress σ_p in MPa (black stars, R = 0.957) and peak strain ε_p (red circles, R = 0.911); and (c) steady-state stress σ_s in MPa (black stars, R = 0.956) and steady-state strain ε_s (red circles, R = 0.922). All datasets are plotted against lnZ on the x-axis. Both characteristic stresses and strains monotonically increase with increasing lnZ, reflecting higher flow resistance and greater strain accumulation under lower temperatures and higher strain rates. The stress curves exhibit nonlinear upward trends, while the strain dependencies are approximately linear. These strong correlations demonstrate that the Z parameter successfully unifies the thermal and mechanical processing variables, enabling constitutive modeling of dynamic recrystallization kinetics for this steel.

This figure presents the dynamic recrystallization (DRX) state diagram for low-density high-strength Fe-Mn-Al-C steel under hot deformation, plotting the natural logarithm of the Zener-Hollomon parameter (lnZ) against true strain (ε). The diagram is divided into three distinct regimes: Region A (work hardening), Region B (partial DRX), and Region C (complete DRX). Black circles connected by an upward curve represent the critical strain (εc) for DRX initiation, separating Region A from Region B. Red stars fitted with a straight line denote the steady-state strain (εs), marking the boundary between Region B and Region C. The figure demonstrates that both εc and εs increase with increasing Z parameter, meaning higher strain rates or lower deformation temperatures shift the DRX boundaries to higher strains. Consequently, the initiation and completion of DRX become more difficult as lnZ rises. Based on hot compression tests conducted at 900–1150 °C and strain rates of 0.01–10 s⁻¹, this reproducible state diagram serves as a predictive tool for mapping microstructure evolution and optimizing thermomechanical processing parameters for Fe-Mn-Al-C steel.

The figure presents a schematic cross-sectional diagram of a compressed Fe-Mn-Al-C steel sample illustrating the heterogeneous deformation distribution induced by friction between the contact surface and the compression platen during hot deformation. The specimen contour is shown with dashed internal boundaries demarcating three distinct zones: Zone 1 (difficult deformation zone) occupies the top and bottom central regions near the contact surfaces; Zone 2 (small deformation zone) is located at the left and right end regions; and Zone 3 (large deformation zone) comprises the central core and horizontal mid-section. A downward loading force P is indicated at the right. The caption and text state that these zones arise from uneven stress distribution during isothermal constant strain rate compression on a Gleeble-3500 simulator. This partitioning is foundational for subsequent microstructural analysis, as the degree of dynamic recrystallization and grain size evolution follows the order Zone 3 > Zone 2 > Zone 1, necessitating zone-specific metallographic observation to accurately characterize the DRX behavior across the sample section.

This figure presents six sets of optical micrographs (a–f) illustrating the microstructure evolution of Fe-Mn-Al-C steel under different isothermal hot compression conditions. Each subset contains three deformation zones: Zone 1 (difficult deformation), Zone 2 (small deformation), and Zone 3 (large deformation). The tested conditions are caption-reported as (a) 1100 °C at 0.01 s⁻¹, (b) 1100 °C at 0.1 s⁻¹, (c) 1100 °C at 10 s⁻¹, (d) 950 °C at 1 s⁻¹, (e) 1050 °C at 1 s⁻¹, and (f) 1150 °C at 1 s⁻¹. Visually, Zone 1 consistently displays coarse equiaxed prior grains with annealing twins and minimal dynamic recrystallization (DRX). Zone 2 shows transitional microstructures with partially elongated and necklace-shaped grains. Zone 3 exhibits the highest DRX extent, evolving from inhomogeneous mixed grains at 950 °C / 1 s⁻¹ to markedly fine, equiaxed recrystallized grains under intermediate conditions, and noticeable grain coarsening at 1150 °C / 0.01 s⁻¹. The comparison across zones and conditions supports that DRX completeness increases from Zone 1 to Zone 3, and that specific temperature–strain rate windows yield fine, uniform DRX structures while extremes produce either insufficient recrystallization or abnormal grain growth.

This figure presents a line chart illustrating the dynamic recrystallization (DRX) average grain size of a low-density high-strength Fe-Mn-Al-C steel as a function of deformation temperature (900–1150 °C) at four constant strain rates: 0.01, 0.1, 1, and 10 s⁻¹. The four curves, distinguished by black squares, red circles, blue triangles, and magenta stars in the legend, respectively, demonstrate that the DRX grain size increases monotonically with decreasing strain rate and increasing temperature. At the most severe condition of 1150 °C and 0.01 s⁻¹, the grain size exceeds 70 μm, indicating rapid grain growth and a coarse-grained structure. Conversely, at lower temperatures (900–950 °C) and higher strain rates (1–10 s⁻¹), the grains remain small (approximately 4–12 μm), but DRX is insufficient, leaving remnant original large grains and yielding poor microstructural homogeneity. These data support the development of a grain size prediction model and identify optimal hot working windows—specifically 950–1000 °C at 0.01–0.1 s⁻¹ and 1050–1100 °C at 1–10 s⁻¹—for achieving fine, uniform, fully recrystallized microstructures.

This figure validates a dynamic recrystallization (DRX) average grain size prediction model for low-density high-strength Fe-Mn-Al-C steel. Panel (a) plots experimental grain size versus deformation temperature from 900 °C to 1150 °C at strain rates of 0.01, 0.1, 1, and 10 s⁻¹, showing that grain size increases with temperature and decreases with increasing strain rate. Panel (b) compares predicted grain size against experimental grain size, where the data points align closely with the linear fit line; the correlation coefficient R is 0.996 and the mean relative error (MRE) is 4.53%, with most points lying within the ±10% deviation bands. Panel (c) shows the absolute error between predicted and experimental values versus experimental grain size, indicating that the majority of errors remain below 3 μm. Together, these results demonstrate that the model accurately predicts DRX grain size under hot deformation conditions, providing a reliable theoretical basis for microstructure control during hot forging.
